skill.name: graphql
skill.description: GraphQL gives clients exactly the data they need - no more, no less. One endpoint, typed schema, introspection. But the flexibility that makes it powerful also makes it dangerous. Without proper controls, GraphQL APIs can face N+1 query problems, deep query DoS attacks, and authorization gaps.
skill.summary: Build secure, performant GraphQL APIs with proper DataLoader usage, query depth limiting, and authorization patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill is a documentation file containing GraphQL best practices and patterns. Static analysis flagged false positives: line 69 references related skills (not shell execution), and lines 3, 22, 34, 63, 72 contain markdown content (not cryptographic code). No executable code or security risks detected.

content.user_title: Build Secure GraphQL APIs with Best Practices
content.value_statement: GraphQL flexibility can lead to performance and security issues without proper controls. This skill provides proven patterns for DataLoader usage, query depth limiting, and authorization to build production-ready GraphQL APIs.
actual_capabilities: Design type-safe GraphQL schemas with proper nullability handling
Implement DataLoader to prevent N+1 query problems
Configure Apollo Client caching with type policies
Set up query depth limiting and complexity analysis
Handle GraphQL subscriptions with proper cleanup
limitations: Focuses on Apollo ecosystem; other implementations may differ
Does not cover federation setup in depth
Production deployment considerations require additional tooling

use_cases.title: Backend Developer Building New API
use_cases.description: Design and implement a production-ready GraphQL API with proper performance optimizations and security controls from the start.

use_cases.title: Frontend Team Integrating GraphQL
use_cases.description: Set up Apollo Client with normalized caching and efficient data fetching patterns for React applications.

use_cases.title: Tech Lead Auditing Existing API
use_cases.description: Identify and fix common GraphQL anti-patterns like missing DataLoader, unlimited query depth, and authorization gaps.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Basic GraphQL Schema
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GraphQL schema for a blog with User, Post, and Comment types. Include proper nullability, relationships, and common CRUD operations as queries and mutations.

prompt_templates.title: Implement DataLoader for N+1 Prevention
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a GraphQL resolver that fetches authors for posts. Each post resolver makes a separate database query. Show me how to implement DataLoader to batch and cache these queries.

prompt_templates.title: Add Query Depth Limiting
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up query depth limiting and complexity analysis for my Apollo Server to prevent DoS attacks from deeply nested GraphQL queries. Include configuration and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Implement Field-Level Authorization
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to implement field-level authorization in GraphQL resolvers. Some fields should only be visible to authenticated users or users with specific roles.

output_examples.input: Design a schema for an e-commerce product catalog
output: GraphQL schema with Product, Category, and Review types
Proper nullability for optional fields like discountPrice
Connections for pagination on product reviews
Mutations for CRUD operations with input types

output_examples.input: Fix N+1 queries in user posts resolver
output: DataLoader implementation batching user ID lookups
Cache keyed by user ID to prevent duplicate queries
Performance improvement from N queries to 1 batched query

best_practices: Always use DataLoader to batch database queries and prevent N+1 problems
Limit query depth and complexity to protect against DoS attacks
Implement authorization in resolvers, not just schema directives
anti_patterns: Making database calls directly in resolvers without DataLoader
Allowing unlimited query depth on your GraphQL endpoint
Relying solely on schema directives for authorization logic

faq.question: Why is DataLoader necessary for GraphQL?
faq.answer: Without DataLoader, each field resolver makes independent database queries. A single query fetching 10 posts with their authors triggers 11 queries (1 for posts, 10 for authors). DataLoader batches these into 2 queries, dramatically improving performance.

faq.question: Should introspection be enabled in production?
faq.answer: Generally no. Introspection exposes your entire schema structure to clients. While useful for development tools, it can help attackers understand your API. Disable it in production unless you specifically need client tooling support.

faq.question: What is the difference between schema and resolver authorization?
faq.answer: Schema directives declare who can access what at the type/field level. Resolver authorization implements the actual logic. Always implement authorization in resolvers; directives alone can be bypassed or misconfigured.

faq.question: How do I handle errors versus null data in GraphQL?
faq.answer: Use proper nullability in your schema. If a field can legitimately be empty, make it nullable. If it must have a value, make it non-null. When a non-null field fails, GraphQL nullifies the entire parent object, which can break queries.

faq.question: What query depth limit should I use?
faq.answer: Start with a depth limit of 5-10 for most APIs. Monitor your legitimate queries and adjust accordingly. Also implement query complexity analysis to catch expensive but shallow queries that bypass depth limits.

faq.question: Do I need to clean up GraphQL subscriptions?
faq.answer: Yes. Subscriptions maintain open connections. When clients disconnect, ensure server-side subscription handlers are properly cleaned up to prevent memory leaks. Implement cleanup in your subscription resolvers using proper lifecycle hooks.
